 {"id":"bd-2zf","title":"Implement IndeterminateSpinner for unknown-length operations","description":"## Purpose
Create a spinner renderable for operations where we do not know the total count or duration, showing activity with elapsed time.

## Background
Many database operations have unknown duration:
- Establishing connections
- Running complex queries
- Waiting for locks
- Initial data discovery

Users need feedback that something is happening, even without a completion percentage.

## Implementation Details

### File Location
crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/spinner.rs

### Core Struct
IndeterminateSpinner holds:
- message: String
- started_at: Instant
- style: SpinnerStyle enum
- theme: Theme

### SpinnerStyle Enum
- Dots: ... (three dots cycling)
- Braille: Unicode braille pattern animation
- Line: -/|\ rotating
- Arrow: arrow rotating
- Simple: asterisk blinking

### Frame Generation
Each style defines frames array and interval_ms.
Example Dots: [".", "..", "...", ".."] at 250ms intervals

### Rendering (Rich Mode)
[...] Connecting to database (2.3s)
^---- spinner animation
     ^---- message
                           ^---- elapsed time

### Plain Text (Agent Mode)
[...] Connecting to database (2.3s)
(Static output, no animation - agents see single line)

## API Design
IndeterminateSpinner::new(message)
  .style(SpinnerStyle::Dots)
  .theme(theme)
  .render(width) / .render_plain()

### Convert to Progress
When length becomes known, convert spinner to progress:
let progress = spinner.into_progress(total);

### Live Update Pattern
let spinner = IndeterminateSpinner::new("Connecting");
console.start_spinner(&spinner);
// ... do work
console.stop_spinner(&spinner);

## Verification Steps
1. Test each spinner style
2. Verify elapsed time formatting (s, m:ss)
3. Test conversion to progress bar
4. Verify plain text output
5. Test with long messages (truncation)
6. Verify animation timing

## Dependencies

 {"id":"bd-318","title":"Phase 8: Facade Crate Integration and Public API","description":"## Purpose
Integrate the console system into the main sqlmodel facade crate, providing a unified public API for console configuration and usage across all sqlmodel components.

## Background
The sqlmodel facade crate is the main entry point for users. It must:
- Re-export console types and traits
- Provide easy console configuration
- Integrate console with high-level operations
- Maintain backward compatibility for users not using console

## Key Deliverables

### 1. Console Re-exports in sqlmodel Prelude
Add to prelude:
- SqlModelConsole
- OutputMode
- Theme
- ConsoleAware trait
- Key renderables (ErrorPanel, QueryResultTable, etc.)

### 2. Console Builder Integration
Extend existing builders to accept console:
Session::builder()
    .with_console(console)
    .connect(url)

### 3. Global Console Support
Optional global console for convenience:
sqlmodel::set_global_console(console);
// Now all operations use this console automatically

### 4. Configuration API
Easy configuration without deep imports:
use sqlmodel::prelude::*;

let console = SqlModelConsole::builder()
    .auto_detect_mode()  // agent vs human
    .theme(Theme::dark())
    .build();

### 5. Feature Flag Coordination
Ensure console feature properly propagates:
- sqlmodel enables sqlmodel-console when console feature active
- Drivers enable their console support when console active
- All is optional, no bloat for non-console users

## Integration Points
- Session/connection builders
- Query execution methods
- Error handling paths
- Schema operations

## Agent Safety Preserved
The facade maintains agent safety guarantees:
- Default is auto-detect mode
- Plain mode always available
- No breaking changes to stdout semantics

## Dependencies
- Phase 2-7 complete (all console infrastructure ready)
- All driver integrations complete

## Verification
- Test prelude imports work
- Test builder integration
- Test global console pattern
- Verify feature flags work correctly","acceptance_criteria":"sqlmodel prelude re-exports console types when feature enabled

 {"id":"bd-3gj","title":"Implement OperationProgress bar for determinate operations","description":"## Purpose
Create a progress bar renderable for operations where we know the total count, showing completion percentage, throughput, and ETA.

## Background
Bulk operations (batch inserts, data exports, migrations) need progress feedback. Users want to see:
- How much is done vs remaining
- Current throughput
- Estimated time to completion
- Running elapsed time

## Implementation Details

### File Location
crates/sqlmodel-console/src/renderables/operation_progress.rs

### Core Struct
OperationProgress holds:
- completed: u64
- total: u64
- started_at: Instant
- operation_name: String
- theme: Theme
- width: Option<usize>
- show_eta: bool
- show_throughput: bool

### Rendering (Rich Mode)
Uses rich_rust ProgressBar with customizations:
- Colored bar based on completion percentage
- Percentage display
- Items processed counter
- Throughput calculation
- ETA based on current rate

Format: Inserting rows [===>    ] 42% (420/1000) 50.2/s ETA: 12s

### Throughput Calculation
rate = completed / elapsed_seconds
eta_seconds = (total - completed) / rate

### Style Variations
- Standard: Blue bar
- Success (100%): Green bar
- Warning (slow): Yellow bar
- Error (stalled): Red bar

### Plain Text (Agent Mode)
Inserting rows: 42% (420/1000) 50.2 rows/s ETA: 12s

## API Design
OperationProgress::new(name, total)
  .completed(n)  // or .increment() / .add(n)
  .theme(theme)
  .show_eta(true)
  .show_throughput(true)
  .width(60)
  .render(width) / .render_plain()

### Update Pattern (for live progress)
let mut progress = OperationProgress::new("Inserting", 1000);
for batch in batches {
    // do work
    progress.add(batch.len());
    console.update_progress(&progress);
}

## Verification Steps
1. Test at 0%, 50%, 100%
2. Verify throughput calculation accuracy
3. Verify ETA reasonableness
4. Test with very fast operations (sub-second)
5. Test with very slow operations (minutes)
6. Verify plain text parseable format
7. Test width constraints

## Dependencies
- rich_rust ProgressBar component
- Theme from this crate
- std::time::Instant for timing","acceptance_criteria":"OperationProgress shows percentage completion

 {"id":"bd-3j0","title":"Add sqlmodel-console to workspace Cargo.toml","description":"# Add sqlmodel-console to Workspace Cargo.toml

## Task Description

Register the new `sqlmodel-console` crate in the workspace root Cargo.toml so it's
recognized as part of the sqlmodel_rust workspace.

## Changes Required

### In /data/projects/sqlmodel_rust/Cargo.toml

Add to the `[workspace]` members list:
```toml
[workspace]
members = [
    "crates/sqlmodel",
    "crates/sqlmodel-core",
    "crates/sqlmodel-macros",
    "crates/sqlmodel-query",
    "crates/sqlmodel-schema",
    "crates/sqlmodel-pool",
    "crates/sqlmodel-postgres",
    "crates/sqlmodel-sqlite",
    "crates/sqlmodel-mysql",
    "crates/sqlmodel-console",  # ADD THIS LINE
]
```

### Optional: Add to Workspace Dependencies

If we want other crates to depend on sqlmodel-console, add:
```toml
[workspace.dependencies]
sqlmodel-console = { path = "crates/sqlmodel-console" }
```

## Verification

```bash
# From workspace root
cargo check --workspace

# Specifically check the new crate
cargo check -p sqlmodel-console

# Verify it appears in workspace
cargo metadata --format-version=1 | jq '.workspace_members[] | select(contains("sqlmodel-console"))'
```

## Important Notes

- The workspace Cargo.toml is the source of truth for workspace membership
- Order in members list doesn't matter but alphabetical is nice
- Don't add features to workspace.dependencies yet; let each crate specify what it needs
- This task cannot be done until the crate directory and Cargo.toml exist

## Why This Matters

Without workspace registration:
- `cargo check --workspace` won't include the crate
- `cargo test --workspace` won't run its tests
- IDE tooling may not recognize it
- Other crates can't depend on it via workspace path","acceptance_criteria":"sqlmodel-console added to workspace members

 {"id":"bd-d7m","title":"Create ErrorPanel renderable in sqlmodel-console","description":"# Create ErrorPanel Renderable in sqlmodel-console

## Task Description

Create an ErrorPanel renderable specifically designed for displaying errors with
rich formatting in Rich mode and structured plain text in Plain mode.

## File: src/renderables/error.rs

```rust
//! Error panel renderable for beautiful error display.

use crate::theme::Theme;

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::prelude::*;

/// Error severity level for styling.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ErrorSeverity {
    /// Critical error - red, urgent
    Critical,
    /// Standard error - red
    Error,
    /// Warning - yellow
    Warning,
    /// Notice - cyan (informational)
    Notice,
}

/// A panel specifically designed for error display.
pub struct ErrorPanel {
    /// Error severity for styling
    severity: ErrorSeverity,
    /// Panel title (e.g., "SQL Syntax Error")
    title: String,
    /// Main error message
    message: String,
    /// Optional SQL query that caused the error
    sql: Option<String>,
    /// Position in SQL where error occurred (1-indexed)
    sql_position: Option<usize>,
    /// SQLSTATE code (PostgreSQL error code)
    sqlstate: Option<String>,
    /// Additional detail from database
    detail: Option<String>,
    /// Hint for fixing the error
    hint: Option<String>,
    /// Additional context lines
    context: Vec<String>,
}

impl ErrorPanel {
    /// Create a new error panel with title and message.
    pub fn new(title: impl Into<String>, message: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        Self {
            severity: ErrorSeverity::Error,
            title: title.into(),
            message: message.into(),
            sql: None,
            sql_position: None,
            sqlstate: None,
            detail: None,
            hint: None,
            context: Vec::new(),
        }
    }
    
    /// Set error severity.
    pub fn severity(mut self, severity: ErrorSeverity) -> Self {
        self.severity = severity;
        self
    }
    
    /// Add SQL query context.
    pub fn with_sql(mut self, sql: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.sql = Some(sql.into());
        self
    }
    
    /// Add error position in SQL (1-indexed character position).
    pub fn with_position(mut self, position: usize) -> Self {
        self.sql_position = Some(position);
        self
    }
    
    /// Add SQLSTATE code.
    pub fn with_sqlstate(mut self, code: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.sqlstate = Some(code.into());
        self
    }
    
    /// Add detail message.
    pub fn with_detail(mut self, detail: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.detail = Some(detail.into());
        self
    }
    
    /// Add hint for fixing.
    pub fn with_hint(mut self, hint: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.hint = Some(hint.into());
        self
    }
    
    /// Add context line.
    pub fn add_context(mut self, line: impl Into<String>) -> Self {
        self.context.push(line.into());
        self
    }
    
    /// Render as rich Panel (feature = "rich").
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_panel(&self, theme: &Theme) -> Panel {
        let border_color = match self.severity {
            ErrorSeverity::Critical => theme.error.to_color(),
            ErrorSeverity::Error => theme.error.to_color(),
            ErrorSeverity::Warning => theme.warning.to_color(),
            ErrorSeverity::Notice => theme.info.to_color(),
        };
        
        let mut content = Text::new(&self.message);
        content.append_line("");
        
        // SQL context with position marker
        if let Some(ref sql) = self.sql {
            content.append_line("┌─ Query ─────────────────────────────────────────┐");
            content.append_line(format!("│ {}", sql));
            
            if let Some(pos) = self.sql_position {
                let marker = format!("│ {}^", " ".repeat(pos.saturating_sub(1)));
                content.append_line(marker);
            }
            content.append_line("└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘");
            content.append_line("");
        }
        
        // Detail
        if let Some(ref detail) = self.detail {
            content.append_line(format!("Detail: {}", detail));
        }
        
        // Hint
        if let Some(ref hint) = self.hint {
            content.append_line("");
            content.append(format!("💡 Hint: {}", hint), theme.info.to_style());
        }
        
        // SQLSTATE
        if let Some(ref code) = self.sqlstate {
            content.append_line("");
            content.append(format!("SQLSTATE: {}", code), theme.dim.to_style());
        }
        
        // Context
        for line in &self.context {
            content.append_line(line);
        }
        
        Panel::from_rich_text(content)
            .title(&self.title)
            .title_style(Style::new().bold().color(border_color.clone()))
            .border_style(Style::new().color(border_color))
    }
    
    /// Render as plain text.
    pub fn to_plain(&self) -> String {
        let mut lines = Vec::new();
        
        lines.push(format!("=== {} ===", self.title));
        lines.push(String::new());
        lines.push(self.message.clone());
        
        if let Some(ref sql) = self.sql {
            lines.push(String::new());
            lines.push("Query:".to_string());
            lines.push(format!("  {}", sql));
            
            if let Some(pos) = self.sql_position {
                lines.push(format!("  {}^", " ".repeat(pos.saturating_sub(1))));
            }
        }
        
        if let Some(ref detail) = self.detail {
            lines.push(String::new());
            lines.push(format!("Detail: {}", detail));
        }
        
        if let Some(ref hint) = self.hint {
            lines.push(String::new());
            lines.push(format!("Hint: {}", hint));
        }
        
        if let Some(ref code) = self.sqlstate {
            lines.push(String::new());
            lines.push(format!("SQLSTATE: {}", code));
        }
        
        for line in &self.context {
            lines.push(line.clone());
        }
        
        lines.join("\n")
    }
    
    /// Render as JSON-serializable structure.
    pub fn to_json(&self) -> serde_json::Value {
        serde_json::json!({
            "severity": format!("{:?}", self.severity),
            "title": self.title,
            "message": self.message,
            "sql": self.sql,
            "position": self.sql_position,
            "sqlstate": self.sqlstate,
            "detail": self.detail,
            "hint": self.hint,
            "context": self.context,
        })
    }
}
```

## Usage Example

```rust
let panel = ErrorPanel::new("SQL Syntax Error", "Unexpected token 'SELCT'")
    .with_sql("SELCT * FROM users WHERE id = $1")
    .with_position(1)
    .with_sqlstate("42601")
    .with_hint("Did you mean 'SELECT'?");

// Rich mode
console.print_renderable(&panel.to_panel(&theme));

// Plain mode
println!("{}", panel.to_plain());
```

cargo check -p sqlmodel-console --features rich
cargo test -p sqlmodel-console renderables::error::tests
cargo run --example error_demo --features rich

 {"id":"bd-eqb","title":"[EPIC] Rich Rust Integration - Premium Console Output","description":"# Epic: Rich Rust Integration

## Executive Summary

Integrate the `rich_rust` library throughout sqlmodel_rust to provide stunning, professional 
console output for humans watching agents work, while ensuring zero interference with AI 
coding agents parsing output.

## Background & Motivation

### The Problem
Currently, sqlmodel_rust has no standardized console output. When users or observers watch 
database operations, they see raw text without visual hierarchy, color-coding, progress 
indicators, clear error presentation, or schema visualization.

### The Opportunity  
The `rich_rust` library (at /dp/rich_rust) provides Python Rich's ergonomic API in Rust:
- Markup syntax: `[bold red]text[/]`
- Tables with auto-sizing columns
- Panels with borders and titles
- Trees for hierarchical data
- Progress bars and spinners
- Syntax highlighting (SQL!)
- Zero unsafe code

### Why This Matters
1. **Developer Experience**: Beautiful output makes the library feel premium
2. **Debugging**: Styled errors with SQL context help fix issues faster
3. **Observability**: Humans watching agents quickly understand what's happening
4. **Differentiation**: No other Rust ORM has this level of console polish

## Critical Constraint: Agent Safety

**Primary users are AI coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, etc.)**

Solution: Dual-Mode Output System with automatic agent detection.
- Agents detected via env vars (CLAUDE_CODE, CODEX_CLI, etc.) → Plain mode
- Humans on TTY without agent markers → Rich mode
- Stream separation: stdout=semantic data, stderr=decorative

## Scope

### In Scope
- New `sqlmodel-console` crate with rich_rust integration
- Mode detection and automatic switching  
- Styled output for all 7 existing crates
- Error panels, query tables, schema trees, pool dashboards
- Progress bars, SQL syntax highlighting
- Comprehensive testing, documentation

### Out of Scope
- Interactive TUI, HTML export, live updating, async rendering

## Success Criteria
1. Agent Compatibility: All existing workflows unchanged
2. Visual Quality: Matches or exceeds Python Rich
3. Zero Default Impact: Optional feature, no deps unless enabled
4. Performance: <1ms overhead per render
5. Coverage: Every user-facing output has styled variant

```rust
//! Query result table renderable.

use crate::theme::Theme;
use sqlmodel_core::{Row, Value};
use std::time::Duration;

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::prelude::*;

/// Options for query result display.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct QueryResultOptions {
    /// Maximum rows to display (None = all)
    pub max_rows: Option<usize>,
    /// Maximum column width before truncation
    pub max_column_width: usize,
    /// Show row numbers
    pub show_row_numbers: bool,
    /// Truncation indicator
    pub truncation_indicator: String,
    /// Show timing information
    pub show_timing: bool,
    /// Plain mode output format
    pub plain_format: PlainFormat,
}

/// Output format for plain mode.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Default)]
pub enum PlainFormat {
    /// Pipe-delimited: id|name|value
    #[default]
    Pipe,
    /// CSV with proper quoting
    Csv,
    /// JSON Lines (one object per row)
    JsonLines,
    /// JSON array of objects
    JsonArray,
}

impl Default for QueryResultOptions {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            max_rows: Some(100),
            max_column_width: 50,
            show_row_numbers: false,
            truncation_indicator: "...".to_string(),
            show_timing: true,
            plain_format: PlainFormat::Pipe,
        }
    }
}

/// A table renderable for query results.
pub struct QueryResultTable {
    /// Column names
    columns: Vec<String>,
    /// Row data
    rows: Vec<Vec<Value>>,
    /// Query execution time
    timing: Option<Duration>,
    /// Display options
    options: QueryResultOptions,
}

impl QueryResultTable {
    /// Create from column names and rows.
    pub fn new(columns: Vec<String>, rows: Vec<Vec<Value>>) -> Self {
        Self {
            columns,
            rows,
            timing: None,
            options: QueryResultOptions::default(),
        }
    }
    
    /// Create from sqlmodel_core Rows.
    pub fn from_rows(column_names: &[String], rows: &[Row]) -> Self {
        let data: Vec<Vec<Value>> = rows.iter()
            .map(|row| row.values().cloned().collect())
            .collect();
        Self::new(column_names.to_vec(), data)
    }
    
    /// Set query timing.
    pub fn with_timing(mut self, timing: Duration) -> Self {
        self.timing = Some(timing);
        self
    }
    
    /// Set display options.
    pub fn with_options(mut self, options: QueryResultOptions) -> Self {
        self.options = options;
        self
    }
    
    /// Set plain format.
    pub fn plain_format(mut self, format: PlainFormat) -> Self {
        self.options.plain_format = format;
        self
    }
    
    /// Row count.
    pub fn row_count(&self) -> usize {
        self.rows.len()
    }
    
    /// Render as rich Table.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_table(&self, theme: &Theme) -> Panel {
        let mut table = Table::new();
        
        // Add columns
        for col in &self.columns {
            table = table.with_column(
                Column::new(col)
                    .header_style(theme.header.to_style().bold())
            );
        }
        
        // Add rows (respecting max_rows)
        let display_rows = match self.options.max_rows {
            Some(max) => self.rows.iter().take(max).collect::<Vec<_>>(),
            None => self.rows.iter().collect::<Vec<_>>(),
        };
        
        for row_data in &display_rows {
            let mut row = rich_rust::Row::new();
            for value in *row_data {
                let (text, style) = self.format_value(value, theme);
                row = row.cell(Cell::new(&text).style(style));
            }
            table.add_row(row);
        }
        
        // Build header with timing
        let header = if let Some(timing) = self.timing {
            format!(
                "{} rows in {:.2}ms",
                self.rows.len(),
                timing.as_secs_f64() * 1000.0
            )
        } else {
            format!("{} rows", self.rows.len())
        };
        
        // Truncation notice
        let footer = if self.options.max_rows.map_or(false, |m| self.rows.len() > m) {
            Some(format!(
                "... and {} more rows",
                self.rows.len() - self.options.max_rows.unwrap()
            ))
        } else {
            None
        };
        
        let mut panel = Panel::new(table)
            .title("Query Results")
            .subtitle(&header);
        
        if let Some(f) = footer {
            panel = panel.subtitle_bottom(&f);
        }
        
        panel
    }
    
    /// Format a value for display with styling.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    fn format_value(&self, value: &Value, theme: &Theme) -> (String, Style) {
        match value {
            Value::Null => ("NULL".to_string(), theme.null_value.to_style().italic()),
            Value::Bool(b) => (b.to_string(), theme.bool_value.to_style()),
            Value::TinyInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::SmallInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Int(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::BigInt(n) => (n.to_string(), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Float(f) => (format!("{:.4}", f), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Double(d) => (format!("{:.6}", d), theme.number_value.to_style()),
            Value::Text(s) => (
                self.truncate_string(s),
                theme.string_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Bytes(b) => (
                format!("[BLOB: {} bytes]", b.len()),
                theme.binary_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Date(_) | Value::Time(_) | Value::Timestamp(_) | Value::TimestampTz(_) => (
                format!("{}", value),
                theme.date_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Uuid(bytes) => (
                format_uuid(bytes),
                theme.uuid_value.to_style()
            ),
            Value::Json(j) => (
                self.truncate_string(&j.to_string()),
                theme.json_value.to_style()
            ),
            _ => (format!("{}", value), Style::new()),
        }
    }
    
    fn truncate_string(&self, s: &str) -> String {
        if s.len() > self.options.max_column_width {
            format!(
                "{}{}",
                &s[..self.options.max_column_width - self.options.truncation_indicator.len()],
                self.options.truncation_indicator
            )
        } else {
            s.to_string()
        }
    }
    
    /// Render as plain text.
    pub fn to_plain(&self) -> String {
        match self.options.plain_format {
            PlainFormat::Pipe => self.to_pipe_delimited(),
            PlainFormat::Csv => self.to_csv(),
            PlainFormat::JsonLines => self.to_json_lines(),
            PlainFormat::JsonArray => self.to_json_array(),
        }
    }
    
    fn to_pipe_delimited(&self) -> String {
        let mut lines = Vec::new();
        lines.push(self.columns.join("|"));
        for row in &self.rows {
            let values: Vec<String> = row.iter()
                .map(|v| format!("{}", v))
                .collect();
            lines.push(values.join("|"));
        }
        lines.join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_csv(&self) -> String {
        let mut lines = Vec::new();
        lines.push(self.columns.iter()
            .map(|c| csv_quote(c))
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join(","));
        for row in &self.rows {
            let values: Vec<String> = row.iter()
                .map(|v| csv_quote(&format!("{}", v)))
                .collect();
            lines.push(values.join(","));
        }
        lines.join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_json_lines(&self) -> String {
        self.rows.iter()
            .map(|row| {
                let obj: serde_json::Map<String, serde_json::Value> = 
                    self.columns.iter().zip(row.iter())
                        .map(|(col, val)| (col.clone(), value_to_json(val)))
                        .collect();
                serde_json::to_string(&obj).unwrap_or_default()
            })
            .collect::<Vec<_>>()
            .join("\n")
    }
    
    fn to_json_array(&self) -> String {
        let arr: Vec<serde_json::Map<String, serde_json::Value>> = self.rows.iter()
            .map(|row| {
                self.columns.iter().zip(row.iter())
                    .map(|(col, val)| (col.clone(), value_to_json(val)))
                    .collect()
            })
            .collect();
        serde_json::to_string_pretty(&arr).unwrap_or_default()
    }
}

fn csv_quote(s: &str) -> String {
    if s.contains(',') || s.contains('"') || s.contains('\n') {
        format!("\"{}\"", s.replace('"', "\"\""))
    } else {
        s.to_string()
    }
}

fn value_to_json(v: &Value) -> serde_json::Value {
    match v {
        Value::Null => serde_json::Value::Null,
        Value::Bool(b) => serde_json::Value::Bool(*b),
        Value::BigInt(n) => serde_json::json!(n),
        Value::Int(n) => serde_json::json!(n),
        Value::Double(d) => serde_json::json!(d),
        Value::Text(s) => serde_json::Value::String(s.clone()),
        _ => serde_json::Value::String(format!("{}", v)),
    }
}

fn format_uuid(bytes: &[u8; 16]) -> String {
    format!(
        "{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}-{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}{:02x}",
        bytes[0], bytes[1], bytes[2], bytes[3],
        bytes[4], bytes[5], bytes[6], bytes[7],
        bytes[8], bytes[9], bytes[10], bytes[11],
        bytes[12], bytes[13], bytes[14], bytes[15]
    )
}
```

Add rich console output to the PostgreSQL driver for connection progress, authentication feedback, and query visualization.

## Background
PostgreSQL connections involve multiple steps:
1. DNS resolution
2. TCP connection
3. SSL/TLS negotiation (optional)
4. Authentication (password, SCRAM-SHA-256, etc)
5. Parameter negotiation

Each step can be visualized to show progress and help debug connection issues.

## Implementation Details

### File Modifications
crates/sqlmodel-postgres/src/connection.rs
crates/sqlmodel-postgres/src/lib.rs (re-exports)

### ConsoleAware Implementation
Add optional SqlModelConsole to PostgresConnection:
struct PostgresConnection {
    // existing fields...
    console: Option<Arc<SqlModelConsole>>,
}

impl ConsoleAware for PostgresConnection {
    fn set_console(&mut self, console: Option<Arc<SqlModelConsole>>) {
        self.console = console;
    }
}

### Connection Progress Output
When console is Some and mode is Rich:

Connecting to PostgreSQL...
  [OK] DNS resolved: 192.168.1.100
  [OK] TCP connected
  [..] Negotiating SSL...
  [OK] SSL established (TLS 1.3)
  [..] Authenticating (SCRAM-SHA-256)...
  [OK] Authenticated as user
  [OK] Connected (server: PostgreSQL 15.2)

### Query Timing Output
For queries, optionally show timing:
Query executed in 12.3ms (5 rows returned)

### EXPLAIN Visualization
When EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE is run:
- Parse the explain output
- Render as a tree showing:
  - Node types (Seq Scan, Index Scan, Hash Join)
  - Costs and actual times
  - Row estimates vs actual
  - Highlighting expensive nodes

### COPY Progress
For COPY operations:
- Show progress bar with row count

## Plain Text Mode

Query: 12.3ms, 5 rows

## Task Description

Create a Theme struct that defines the color palette used throughout sqlmodel_rust
console output. This ensures visual consistency across all renderables.

```rust
//! Theme definitions for SQLModel console output.
//!
//! Provides a consistent color palette for all styled output.

#[cfg(feature = "rich")]
use rich_rust::style::{Color, Style};

/// SQLModel console theme with semantic colors.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Theme {
    // === Status Colors ===
    /// Success messages, completion indicators
    pub success: ThemeColor,
    /// Error messages, failure indicators  
    pub error: ThemeColor,
    /// Warning messages, deprecation notices
    pub warning: ThemeColor,
    /// Informational messages, hints
    pub info: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Value Type Colors ===
    /// NULL values (typically dim/italic)
    pub null_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Boolean values (true/false)
    pub bool_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Numeric values (integers, floats)
    pub number_value: ThemeColor,
    /// String/text values
    pub string_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Date/time/timestamp values
    pub date_value: ThemeColor,
    /// Binary/blob values
    pub binary_value: ThemeColor,
    /// JSON values
    pub json_value: ThemeColor,
    /// UUID values
    pub uuid_value: ThemeColor,
    
    // === SQL Syntax Colors ===
    /// SQL keywords (SELECT, FROM, WHERE)
    pub sql_keyword: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL strings ('value')
    pub sql_string: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL numbers (42, 3.14)
    pub sql_number: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL comments (-- comment)
    pub sql_comment: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL operators (=, >, AND)
    pub sql_operator: ThemeColor,
    /// SQL identifiers (table names, column names)
    pub sql_identifier: ThemeColor,
    
    // === UI Element Colors ===
    /// Table/panel borders
    pub border: ThemeColor,
    /// Headers and titles
    pub header: ThemeColor,
    /// Dimmed/secondary text
    pub dim: ThemeColor,
    /// Highlighted/emphasized text
    pub highlight: ThemeColor,
}

/// A color that can be rendered differently based on mode.
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct ThemeColor {
    /// RGB color for rich mode (r, g, b)
    pub rgb: (u8, u8, u8),
    /// ANSI 256-color fallback
    pub ansi256: u8,
    /// Plain text marker (for plain mode output)
    pub plain_marker: Option<&'static str>,
}

impl ThemeColor {
    /// Create a theme color with RGB and ANSI256 fallback.
    pub const fn new(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: None }
    }
    
    /// Create with a plain text marker for non-color output.
    pub const fn with_marker(rgb: (u8, u8, u8), ansi256: u8, marker: &'static str) -> Self {
        Self { rgb, ansi256, plain_marker: Some(marker) }
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Color (truecolor).
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_color(&self) -> Color {
        Color::from_rgb(self.rgb.0, self.rgb.1, self.rgb.2)
    }
    
    /// Convert to rich_rust Style with this as foreground.
    #[cfg(feature = "rich")]
    pub fn to_style(&self) -> Style {
        Style::new().color(self.to_color())
    }
}

impl Theme {
    /// Create the default dark theme (Dracula-inspired).
    pub fn dark() -> Self {
        Self {
            // Status colors (Dracula palette)
            success: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),      // Green
            error: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),        // Red
            warning: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),    // Yellow
            info: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117),       // Cyan
            
            // Value type colors
            null_value: ThemeColor::with_marker((98, 114, 164), 60, "NULL"),
            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((241, 250, 140), 228),  // Yellow
            number_value: ThemeColor::new((139, 233, 253), 117), // Cyan
            string_value: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),   // Green
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),   // Magenta
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215), // Orange
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 184, 108), 215),   // Orange
            
            // SQL syntax colors
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((255, 121, 198), 212),  // Magenta
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((80, 250, 123), 84),     // Green
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((189, 147, 249), 141),   // Purple
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),    // Gray
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((255, 85, 85), 203),   // Red
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255), // White
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),        // Gray
            header: ThemeColor::new((248, 248, 242), 255),      // White
            dim: ThemeColor::new((98, 114, 164), 60),           // Gray
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((255, 255, 255), 231),   // Bright white
        }
    }
    
    /// Create a light theme variant.
    pub fn light() -> Self {
        Self {

            success: ThemeColor::new((40, 167, 69), 34),
            error: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            warning: ThemeColor::new((255, 193, 7), 220),

            // Value colors (darker for visibility on light bg)

            bool_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),

            string_value: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            date_value: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            binary_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            json_value: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            uuid_value: ThemeColor::new((255, 152, 0), 208),
            
            // SQL syntax (darker)
            sql_keyword: ThemeColor::new((156, 39, 176), 128),
            sql_string: ThemeColor::new((76, 175, 80), 34),
            sql_number: ThemeColor::new((103, 58, 183), 92),
            sql_comment: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            sql_operator: ThemeColor::new((220, 53, 69), 160),
            sql_identifier: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            
            // UI elements
            border: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            header: ThemeColor::new((33, 37, 41), 235),
            dim: ThemeColor::new((108, 117, 125), 244),
            highlight: ThemeColor::new((0, 0, 0), 16),
        }
    }
}

impl Default for Theme {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self::dark()
    }
}
```

## Color Philosophy

### Dracula Palette Base
The dark theme uses colors from the Dracula palette because:
1. It's widely recognized and loved by developers
2. High contrast ratios for accessibility
3. Distinct colors for each semantic meaning
4. Looks professional in terminal screenshots

### Semantic Consistency
- **Green** = Success, strings (positive/data)
- **Red** = Errors, operators (danger/action)
- **Yellow** = Warnings, booleans (caution/special)
- **Cyan** = Info, numbers (neutral data)
- **Magenta** = Dates, SQL keywords (special syntax)
- **Purple** = JSON, SQL numbers (structured data)
- **Gray** = Dim text, comments, borders (secondary)

### Accessibility
- All colors chosen to meet WCAG contrast guidelines
- ANSI256 fallbacks for terminals without truecolor
- Plain markers for complete accessibility

-{"id":"sqlmodel_rust-0gv","title":"SQLModel Rust: MySQL Protocol Implementation","description":"# Epic: MySQL Protocol Implementation (sqlmodel-mysql)

## Overview
This epic implements the MySQL wire protocol from scratch. MySQL uses a different protocol than PostgreSQL but follows similar principles: TCP connection, length-prefixed packets, and request-response patterns.

## Rationale
Same reasoning as PostgreSQL: full control, native async, cancellation support, and first-principles understanding. MySQL is important for:
- Huge existing deployments
- AWS Aurora MySQL
- MariaDB compatibility
- Different feature set than PostgreSQL

## Key Components

### 1. Packet Protocol (protocol.rs)
MySQL uses 4-byte header packets:
- 3 bytes: payload length
- 1 byte: sequence number
- Payload

Packets over 16MB are split. Sequence numbers track request/response pairing.

### 2. Connection Handshake (handshake.rs)
Initial handshake:
1. Server sends HandshakeV10:
   - Protocol version
   - Server version string
   - Connection ID
   - Auth plugin data (salt)
   - Capability flags
   - Character set
   - Status flags
2. Client sends HandshakeResponse:
   - Capability flags
   - Max packet size
   - Character set
   - Username, auth response
   - Database name
   - Auth plugin name

### 3. Authentication (auth.rs)
MySQL auth plugins:
- mysql_native_password: SHA1-based (legacy)
- caching_sha2_password: SHA256-based (default MySQL 8.0+)
- sha256_password: RSA-based
- auth_gssapi_client: Kerberos

caching_sha2_password flow:
1. Client sends scrambled password
2. Server may request full auth (public key exchange)
3. Client encrypts password with server's public key
4. Server verifies

### 4. Command Protocol (commands.rs)
MySQL commands (COM_xxx):
- COM_QUERY: Text protocol query
- COM_STMT_PREPARE: Prepare statement
- COM_STMT_EXECUTE: Execute prepared
- COM_STMT_CLOSE: Close prepared
- COM_STMT_RESET: Reset statement
- COM_PING: Keep-alive
- COM_QUIT: Close connection
- COM_INIT_DB: Change database
- COM_FIELD_LIST: Get columns (deprecated)

### 5. Result Sets (results.rs)
Text protocol results:
1. Column count packet
2. Column definition packets (per column)
3. EOF packet (or OK if no results)
4. Row packets (length-encoded strings)
5. EOF packet (end of rows)

Binary protocol results (prepared statements):
- Similar structure but binary encoding
- NULL bitmap for nullable columns
- Type-specific binary encoding

### 6. Type System (types.rs)
MySQL types to Rust:
- MYSQL_TYPE_TINY -> i8
- MYSQL_TYPE_SHORT -> i16
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONG -> i32
- MYSQL_TYPE_LONGLONG -> i64
- MYSQL_TYPE_FLOAT -> f32
- MYSQL_TYPE_DOUBLE -> f64
- MYSQL_TYPE_STRING, VARCHAR -> String
- MYSQL_TYPE_BLOB -> Vec<u8>
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATE -> date
- MYSQL_TYPE_DATETIME -> datetime
- MYSQL_TYPE_JSON -> serde_json::Value

Length-encoded integers:
- < 251: 1 byte
- < 2^16: 0xFC + 2 bytes
- < 2^24: 0xFD + 3 bytes
- < 2^64: 0xFE + 8 bytes

### 7. Prepared Statements (prepared.rs)
Binary protocol advantages:
- No SQL injection (parameters separate)
- Efficient binary encoding
- Server-side caching
- Correct type handling

Flow:
1. COM_STMT_PREPARE -> statement ID, param count, column count
2. COM_STMT_EXECUTE with binary parameters
3. Binary result set

### 8. Error Handling (error.rs)
ERR packet format:
- 0xFF marker
- Error code (2 bytes)
- SQL state marker '#'
- SQL state (5 bytes)
- Error message

Map MySQL error codes to meaningful errors.

### 9. Transactions
MySQL transaction commands:
- START TRANSACTION
- COMMIT, ROLLBACK
- SAVEPOINT, ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT
- SET autocommit = 0/1

### 10. Character Sets
MySQL character set handling:
- Connection charset
- Result charset
- Collation

Default to utf8mb4 for full Unicode.

## Key Design Decisions
1. **Async native**: Use asupersync TCP throughout
2. **Capability negotiation**: Support both old and new features
3. **Auth plugin system**: Extensible auth mechanism
4. **Prepared statement cache**: Reuse prepared statements
5. **Connection reset**: Use COM_RESET_CONNECTION vs reconnect

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Connect to MySQL 5.7 and 8.0+
- [ ] All auth methods work
- [ ] Text protocol queries
- [ ] Binary protocol prepared statements
- [ ] All common types map correctly
- [ ] Transactions work correctly
- [ ] Error codes mapped to Rust errors
- [ ] SSL/TLS connections
- [ ] Connection pooling integration
- [ ] MariaDB compatibility
- [ ] Correct character set handling

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-core (Connection trait, Value, Row, Error)
- asupersync (TCP, cancellation, Budget)

## Estimated Scope
~2000 lines of protocol implementation

## References
- MySQL Protocol: https://dev.mysql.com/doc/dev/mysql-server/latest/PAGE_PROTOCOL.html
- MariaDB Protocol: https://mariadb.com/kb/en/clientserver-protocol/","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"TealOtter","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T16:19:14.902857954Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-27T07:08:41.946996224Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

## Overview
This epic covers the foundational types and traits that all other components build upon. The core layer defines the contract between the ORM layer and database drivers, providing type-safe abstractions for values, rows, connections, and errors.

## Rationale
In Python SQLModel, the core types are spread across Pydantic (for validation types) and SQLAlchemy (for database types). In Rust, we consolidate these into a single coherent type system that leverages:
- Rust's enum system for type-safe value representation
- Traits for connection abstraction (allowing multiple backends)
- asupersync's Outcome type for cancellation-aware operations

## Key Design Decisions
1. **Value enum over dynamic typing**: Unlike Python's runtime type coercion, we use a comprehensive Value enum that covers all SQL types with explicit conversions
2. **Row as indexed + named access**: Support both positional (index) and named (column) access patterns
3. **Connection trait with async**: All operations are async and take Cx for structured concurrency
4. **Error hierarchy**: Typed errors for different failure modes (connection, query, type conversion)

## Success Criteria
- [ ] All SQL primitive types representable in Value enum
- [ ] Bidirectional conversion between Rust types and Value
- [ ] Connection trait supports query, execute, transaction
- [ ] Full asupersync integration (Cx, Outcome, cancellation)
- [ ] Comprehensive error types with context
- [ ] Zero unsafe code in public API

## Dependencies
- asupersync crate (external)
- serde for serialization traits

## Context
The Value enum is the universal representation for all SQL values in SQLModel Rust. It must support every SQL type we need while maintaining type safety and efficient conversion.

## Current State
Basic Value enum exists with: Null, Bool, TinyInt, SmallInt, Integer, BigInt, Real, Double, Text, Blob.

## Required Enhancements

### Additional Variants Needed
```rust
pub enum Value {
    // Existing
    Null,
    Bool(bool),
    TinyInt(i8),
    SmallInt(i16),
    Integer(i32),
    BigInt(i64),
    Real(f32),
    Double(f64),
    Text(String),
    Blob(Vec<u8>),
    
    // Add these
    Decimal(String),           // Exact decimal (stored as string for precision)
    Date(i32),                 // Days since Unix epoch
    Time(i64),                 // Microseconds since midnight
    DateTime(i64),             // Microseconds since Unix epoch
    Timestamp(i64),            // Same as DateTime but with timezone awareness flag
```

 {"id":"sqlmodel_rust-53e.2","title":"Core: Implement Row with efficient column access","description":"# Task: Implement Row with Efficient Column Access

## Context
Row represents a database result row. It must support both positional access (by index) and named access (by column name) efficiently, as query results can be large.

## Current State
Basic Row struct exists with HashMap for columns. Performance may be suboptimal for large result sets.

## Required Enhancements

### Optimized Storage
```rust
pub struct Row {
    /// Column values in order
    values: Vec<Value>,
    /// Column names (shared across rows in same result set)
    columns: Arc<ColumnInfo>,
}

pub struct ColumnInfo {
    /// Column names in order
    names: Vec<String>,
    /// Name -> index mapping for O(1) lookup
    name_to_index: HashMap<String, usize>,
}
```

### Key Benefits
1. **Shared column info**: All rows from same query share one ColumnInfo via Arc
2. **O(1) name lookup**: HashMap for name-to-index
3. **O(1) index access**: Direct Vec access
4. **Memory efficient**: No string duplication per row

### API
```rust
impl Row {
    /// Get value by index (O(1))
    pub fn get(&self, index: usize) -> Option<&Value>;
    
    /// Get value by column name (O(1) via HashMap)
    pub fn get_named(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&Value>;
    
    /// Get and convert by index
    pub fn get_as<T: FromValue>(&self, index: usize) -> Result<T>;
    
    /// Get and convert by name
    pub fn get_named_as<T: FromValue>(&self, name: &str) -> Result<T>;
    
    /// Number of columns
    pub fn len(&self) -> usize;
    
    /// Column names iterator
    pub fn column_names(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = &str>;
    
    /// Values iterator
    pub fn values(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = &Value>;
    
    /// Iterate as (name, value) pairs
    pub fn iter(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = (&str, &Value)>;
    
    /// Check if column exists
    pub fn contains_column(&self, name: &str) -> bool;
}
```
